calendar.matters:
Notice Of Hearing On Petition To Compel Arbitration
Off Calendar Apr-18-2022 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE:
Ann Surapruik, Esq. (510.337.1001; ASurapruik@unioncounsel.net) of Weinberg, Roger & Rosenfeld for Petitioner.
Erik Rapoport, Esq. (415.554.3950; Erik.Rapoport@sfcityatty.org) Deputy City Attorney for Respondent.

ARGUED (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript). The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PETITIONER SERVICE EMPLOYEES INTERNATIONAL UNION, LOCAL 1021'S Petition To Compel Arbitration.

Service Employees International Union, Local 1021's petition to compel arbitration is granted.

The union had a collective bargaining agreement with the City and County of San Francisco that began in July 2014 and ended in June 2019; this was followed by a CBA that began in July 2019 and ends in June 2022. Both CBAs provide for grievance arbitration.

In 2014, the City agreed with the union to pay $4,000 bonuses for four job classifications in which retention was problematic. (Opp. 4:9-18.) The City says these bonuses ended in 2019 when it moved employees into new classifications. (Id. at 4:26-5:14.) The union disagreed, grieved and initiated an arbitration. (Id. at 5:25-26.) The City seemed to agree to arbitrate by working with the union to select arbitrator Joel Schaffer. (Memo, 2:10-15.) However, when Schaffer tried to schedule the arbitration, the City said the grievance was not arbitrable. (Id.)

Both parties spend much of their briefing arguing their dispute's merits, but that is not what the pending petition calls for me to decide. Rather, the issue is whether the parties have an agreement to arbitrate. (See Banner Entertainment v. Sup. Ct. (1998) 62 Cal.App.4th 348, 356.) They do: both CBAs provide for grievance arbitration.

The City says no arbitration agreement exists because the "retention bonuses [are] for job classifications that are no longer operable." (Opp. 6:22-23.) This is circular reasoning; it assumes the truth of the City's position on a key issue to be arbitrated.

Order is signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr., Clerk: S. Le, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/RBU)
